expectations.text: behavioral_check: Filters on `region_name` from `BrainRegion` (a secondary varchar column, NOT a PK) and walks through `Electrode` (which FKs to BrainRegion per common_ephys.py:79) to identify sessions with PFC electrodes
expectations.evidence: Restricts BrainRegion via `region_name LIKE '%refrontal%' OR region_name LIKE '%PFC%'` and walks ElectrodeGroup → BrainRegion (also notes Electrode equivalent).

expectations.text: behavioral_check: Routes the w-track restriction PRIMARILY through `Task.task_name` and/or `TaskEpoch.task_environment` (common_task.py), with `IntervalList.interval_list_name` mentioned only as a fallback / validation clue (the lab-dependent naming convention) — does NOT rely solely on interval-list naming
expectations.evidence: Filters Task by task_name and joins TaskEpoch & wtrack_tasks; never relies on IntervalList naming.

expectations.text: behavioral_check: Projects each side to `subject_id` (via Session's `-> [nullable] Subject` FK) BEFORE intersecting, rather than attempting a single multi-table `*` join across PFC + w-track + subject (which would either trigger dependent-attribute refusal or produce spurious Cartesian products)
expectations.evidence: Computes `subjects_pfc = set(sessions_with_pfc.fetch('subject_id'))` and `subjects_wtrack = set(sessions_with_wtrack.fetch('subject_id'))` then intersects in Python — separate per-side projection.

expectations.text: behavioral_check: Flags both `region_name` substrings and `task_name` / `task_environment` values as lab-dependent conventions to confirm via `Task.fetch('task_name')` etc. before running the filter
expectations.evidence: Step 1 explicitly says both labels are free-text and recommends `BrainRegion.fetch('region_name', ...)` and `Task.fetch('task_name', ...)` before restricting.

expectations.text: behavioral_check: Does NOT claim Session has a `task_name` column directly (it doesn't); the path goes through TaskEpoch -> Task
expectations.evidence: The path is Session ← TaskEpoch → Task; never restricts Session by task_name.
